planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; sweet olive is adaptable but performs best with morning sun and afternoon shade in the hottest climates. It is slow-growing but very long-lived once established.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant olive (sweet olive, tea olive) is one of the most beloved fragrant shrubs of the Gulf South — a classic fixture in old Southern gardens, cherished for its tiny, creamy white flowers that perfume the air with an intense, sweet apricot-like fragrance that can carry 100 yards on a still autumn morning. The plant blooms repeatedly from fall through spring in zone 8–9, often triggered by cool snaps, and the fragrance is one of the most powerful and instantly recognizable in the Southern garden tradition. The large, dense, glossy evergreen shrub makes an outstanding screen, hedge, or specimen. It grows slowly but lives for decades and eventually becomes a small tree. The variety O. fragrans f. aurantiacus has orange flowers; 'Fudingzhu' is a particularly free-flowering white selection.

common_name: Fatsia (Japanese Fatsia, Japanese Aralia, Glossy-Leaf Paper Plant)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall or spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; fatsia is exceptionally shade tolerant and actually performs better in deep shade than in full sun, where the large leaves can bleach and burn in heat.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; fatsia is one of the most dramatic and tropical-looking shade shrubs for zone 7–11 gardens, producing enormous, glossy, deeply lobed leaves 12–18 inches across on bold, multi-stemmed plants that create an instant tropical feel in the darkest garden corners. The leaves hold their rich, dark green color year-round and are remarkably resistant to wind damage. Clusters of small white ball-shaped flowers appear in fall, followed by black berries. It is one of the most shade-tolerant large shrubs available and thrives under the dense canopy of live oaks and other large trees where most plants fail. The variegated form ('Variegata') has attractive white-edged leaves. Equally at home as an outdoor landscape shrub in zone 7–11 and as a bold container plant for covered patios and atriums.

common_name: Aucuba (Japanese Aucuba, Spotted Laurel, Gold Dust Plant)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; aucuba is one of the most shade-tolerant broad-leaved evergreen shrubs available and tolerates dense, dry shade under trees where most plants fail. It is somewhat drought tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; aucuba is an outstanding broad-leaved evergreen shrub for zone 7–9 shade, producing large, leathery, glossy leaves in solid dark green or in the popular spotted 'Variegata' form with irregular yellow speckles that light up deep shade like scattered gold dust. The spotted forms are called 'gold dust plant' and are among the most striking variegated foliage plants for shade. Female plants bear large, glossy red berries in fall and winter that persist attractively; a single male plant is required for fruit production. Aucuba tolerates dense, dry shade under trees better than virtually any other broad-leaved evergreen shrub, surviving in conditions that defeat hostas, ferns, and most other shade plants. It is the ultimate plant for the dry, root-filled shade under large oaks and pines in the Gulf South.

common_name: Leatherleaf Mahonia (Chinese Mahonia, Leatherleaf Oregon Grape)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in well-drained, moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; leatherleaf mahonia is one of the toughest shade shrubs and tolerates dry shade under trees once established. The spiny leaflets make handling difficult — wear gloves.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; leatherleaf mahonia is a bold architectural evergreen shrub with some of the most striking foliage in the shade garden — stiff, upright canes bearing enormous compound leaves (18–24 inches) of dark, holly-like leaflets with viciously spiny edges that create a dramatic tropical effect in the deepest shade. Fragrant yellow flowers appear in winter in drooping grape-like clusters, followed by powdery blue-purple berries in spring that are edible (tart, used for jelly and wine). It is one of the few large shrubs that truly thrives in deep, dry shade — an outstanding barrier plant and shade specimen. NOTE: Mahonia bealei is listed as invasive in several southeastern states including Texas and Georgia — check local invasive species lists before planting; the native alternative Mahonia trifoliolata (agarita) is an excellent substitute in Texas.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; hellebores resent disturbance once established — plant in a permanent location and do not divide unless essential. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial to full shade; lenten rose (Helleborus x hybridus) is one of the finest and most prized of all shade garden perennials — a nearly evergreen perennial that flowers in late winter (February–March in zone 8–9) with nodding, cup-shaped flowers 2–4 inches across in an extraordinary range of colors: white, cream, pale yellow, pink, rose, burgundy, deep plum, near-black, and spotted or picotee bicolors. No other garden plant offers such refined beauty in the dead of winter shade. The leathery, deeply divided leaves persist year-round, creating outstanding year-round texture. In zone 9a, site in deep shade with excellent drainage to avoid summer root rot. It thrives in the Southeast under deciduous trees and is remarkable for blooming when almost nothing else does in deep shade. Deer resistant. All parts toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Plant small rhizomes (bulblets) 1 inch deep in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; purple shamrock grows from small, scaly rhizomes that can be left in the ground year-round in zone 7–10. The plant goes summer-dormant in intense heat and re-emerges in fall.

directions: Plant small rhizomes 1 inch deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; purple shamrock is one of the most charming and versatile small shade plants, producing triangular, folded leaves in deep burgundy-purple with three leaflets that look exactly like large, richly colored shamrocks. The leaves fold downward at night and in strong sun (nyctinasty), 'sleeping' closed before reopening each morning. Small pink flowers are produced on delicate stems above the foliage from spring through fall. It is outstanding in containers, window boxes, and as a low border edging in partial shade. The leaves and flowers are edible with a pleasant sorrel-like tartness (high in oxalic acid — consume only in small amounts). In zone 7–10 the rhizomes overwinter in the ground; the plant may go partly dormant in hot summers and resprout in fall with cooler temperatures.

planting.note: Plant in shallow, very well-drained, peat-based, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in bright indirect shade; rex begonias have shallow root systems and perform best in wide, shallow containers rather than deep pots. They are sensitive to overwatering — the rhizome rots quickly in wet or poorly drained conditions.

directions: Plant in shallow, very well-drained, peat-based soil in bright indirect shade; rex begonias are grown almost exclusively for their spectacular foliage — the large, asymmetric leaves in patterns of silver, green, burgundy, red, pink, purple, copper, and bronze in bold swirling patterns are arguably the most ornamental of any leaf in the plant kingdom. Each cultivar has a unique, artistic pattern that looks almost hand-painted. They are outstanding container plants for shaded patios and covered porches where their foliage can be admired up close. As an outdoor shade garden plant in zone 10–12, they thrive under trees in similar conditions to calatheas and marantas. They prefer high humidity, bright indirect light, and shallow, moist-but-never-soggy soil. The rhizomes can be divided and propagated; individual leaves can also be rooted in moist peat to produce new plants.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container transplants in fall in moist to dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; English ivy is widely adaptable but can be invasive outside cultivated areas — do not plant near forests, natural areas, or unmanaged land. In zone 9a it may struggle in summer heat and drought.

directions: Plant cuttings in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; English ivy is the classic, most widely used shade groundcover in temperate gardens — the glossy, lobed, evergreen leaves in many different sizes and variegations (solid green, silver-variegated, gold-variegated, small-leaved, large-leaved) create a dense, weed-suppressing carpet under trees. It climbs structures by aerial rootlets and has been used for centuries to cover buildings, walls, and slopes. However, English ivy is invasive in many parts of the United States east of the Rockies, smothering native understory plants and climbing trees — where naturalized, it can eventually kill large trees by girdling. Plant only in maintained, contained situations and remove regularly from any areas where it might spread to natural woodland. In zone 9a summer heat and humidity can reduce vigor; the heat-tolerant 'Needlepoint' and small-leaved varieties perform better in the Gulf South.

common_name: Algerian Ivy (Canary Island Ivy, Canariensis Ivy)

planting.note: Root cuttings or plant container transplants in fall or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Algerian ivy is more heat and drought tolerant than English ivy and a better choice for zone 8–10 Gulf Coast shade gardens. It can also be grown as a trailing vine for containers.

directions: Plant cuttings in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Algerian ivy is the superior ivy for Gulf Coast zone 7–10 gardens — larger-leaved, more heat tolerant, and more drought tolerant than English ivy, making it better suited to the summer conditions of the Gulf South. The leaves are 3–8 inches across, rich glossy green (sometimes with a reddish tint in winter), and distinctly larger than the familiar English ivy. The variegated form 'Gloire de Marengo' has silvery gray-green leaves with creamy white margins and is one of the most beautiful of all shade vines. Like English ivy it can climb by aerial roots and spreads vigorously as a groundcover, but has a less aggressive spreading habit than English ivy and is less likely to invade natural areas. Still, plant with care and keep away from forest edges.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; epimediums tolerate dry shade under established trees better than almost any other perennial groundcover. Do not disturb established plantings unnecessarily — they improve dramatically over years.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; epimedium (barrenwort, bishop's hat) is arguably the most underused and underrated of all shade groundcovers — a slowly spreading, low-maintenance perennial that tolerates dry shade under established tree roots better than any other herbaceous perennial. The attractive, heart-shaped leaflets are often mottled with red or bronze when young, and the dainty four-petaled flowers resembling tiny hats or butterflies in yellow, pink, white, red, or bicolor appear in spring. E. x versicolor 'Sulphureum' has pale yellow flowers and reliably semi-evergreen foliage in mild winters. Once established, epimediums are nearly drought proof and essentially pest-free. Cut old foliage to the ground in late winter before new growth begins to showcase the spring flowers. A plant that rewards patience — increasingly impressive each year after establishment.
